hook: You already know how to graph lines like $y = 2x + 3$ by finding the slope and y-intercept. But what happens when a line has NO slope, or when every point on it shares the exact same x-value? Try to graph the equation $x = 4$ — there's no $y$ on the right side, so you can't solve for slope or y-intercept the usual way. What kind of line does this equation even make?
concept: A horizontal line is the graph of every point that shares the same y-value, written $y = k$ for some constant $k$. A vertical line is the graph of every point that shares the same x-value, written $x = h$ for some constant $h$. Horizontal lines have a slope of exactly $0$ because there is no rise, while vertical lines have undefined slope because the run is $0$ and division by zero is undefined.
anatomy: Both special line forms have two key components: the axis variable and the constant. In $y = k$, the axis variable is $y$ — it tells you this line runs parallel to the x-axis — and $k$ is the constant that names the fixed y-value where the line sits (e.g., $k = -3$ means the line crosses the y-axis at $-3$ and stays there forever). In $x = h$, the axis variable is $x$ — signaling a line parallel to the y-axis — and $h$ is the constant that fixes every point's x-coordinate. The slope of a horizontal line is always $m = 0$, while the slope of a vertical line is always described as undefined.

worked_example.description_latex: We start by recognizing the form: this equation has only a $y$ and a constant, with no $x$ at all. That tells us immediately it is a horizontal line, because no matter what x-value you pick, $y$ is always $-2$.

To confirm, we pick any x-values we like and pair each with $y = -2$. Every single point lands on the same horizontal level, which is two units below the x-axis. This pattern of constant y-values is the signature of a horizontal line.

We verify the slope using two of our points: the rise (change in $y$) is $0$ because $y$ never changes, while the run (change in $x$) is $4$. A slope of $0$ confirms this is a perfectly flat, horizontal line.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Draw a straight line through all points at } y = -2, \text{ extending left and right infinitely.}
worked_example.description_latex: We plot the points and draw a solid horizontal line through them, making sure it stretches in both directions. The line crosses the y-axis at the single point $(0, -2)$, which is its y-intercept, and it never crosses the x-axis.

partial_example.description_latex: Look at the form of this equation carefully — it only contains $x$ and a constant, with no $y$ present at all. This tells us it is a vertical line, because every point on the line must have an x-coordinate of exactly $3$, regardless of the y-value.
We generate a few points by keeping $x = 3$ fixed and choosing any y-values we want. Notice that the x-coordinate never changes — only $y$ varies.

Now it's your turn: explain in one sentence why the slope of this line is undefined (hint: try computing $m$ using two of the points above and see what happens in the denominator), then describe where the line crosses the x-axis.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Identify whether the line } y = 7 \text{ is horizontal or vertical, and state its slope.}
\text{Write the equation of the vertical line that passes through the point } (5, -8).
\text{Write the equation of the horizontal line that passes through the point } (-3, 6).

\text{Line } A \text{ passes through } (2, 1) \text{ and } (2, -5). \text{ Line } B \text{ passes through } (-4, 1) \text{ and } (3, 1). \text{ At what point do lines } A \text{ and } B \text{ intersect?}

\text{A city street grid has a road that runs perfectly east–west and passes through the map coordinate } (0, -3). \text{ A second road runs perfectly north–south through } (6, 0). \text{ Write the equation of each road and find where they cross.}
practice_problems.answer_latex: y = -3 \text{ and } x = 6; \text{ they cross at } (6,\,-3)

common_mistakes: Students often confuse which axis a line is parallel to: they see $x = 4$ and think 'x-axis,' but $x = 4$ is actually parallel to the y-axis (vertical). Remember: the variable in the equation names the axis the line is perpendicular to, not the one it runs along.

When asked for the slope of a vertical line $x = h$, students write $m = 0$ (copying from horizontal lines). The fix is to try computing slope with two points — the denominator (run) is always $0$, making the slope undefined, not zero.

Students sometimes write the equation for a line through $(2, 5)$ that is horizontal as $x = 2$ instead of $y = 5$. A horizontal line keeps $y$ constant, so the equation always uses the y-value of the given point, not the x-value.
untested_variants: Determining the equation of a horizontal or vertical line from its graph (reading the constant off a drawn coordinate plane) rather than from a given point.
Finding the area of a rectangle whose four sides are defined by two horizontal lines and two vertical lines (combining distance between parallel lines with area calculation).
Deciding whether a table of values represents a horizontal or vertical line based on repeated x-values or repeated y-values, without an equation given.
Graphing or analyzing a horizontal or vertical line in a real-world context where the domain is restricted, such as $x = 3$ only for $0 \le y \le 10$.
